speaker: interviewee
text: You still need me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that much problem?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind just having a little drink? Because I think your voice is just getting a little bit raspy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some water.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind just having a drink of water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, I have a drink of water. I'm not really that badly off. Am I that bad-- do I really--

speaker: interviewer
text: You look like you're getting warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm getting warm, I know. But do I look--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feeble?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not feeble.

speaker: interviewee
text: No feeble?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I shouldn't have warned him.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're going to find out-- not me. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me exactly what you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six, January 9, 1995. The survivor is Mr. George Meles. Tell me about when you first got to Canada. What was the reaction of the community here-- the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish community was overwhelmed. They thought-- they said the Jews are coming from-- to Toronto from all over Europe, all over the world. They're going to take away our jobs. They are going to take away our-- They are going to make the situation of the Jews worse, because there are so-- we are going to have so many Jews, and the Gentiles are going to rebel. And-- and the government of-- King's government-- was said no Jew is-- no, no Jewish is too much. So that was the attitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have-- we never asked for handout. We never received handout from the Canadian government, or from our relatives, or from the JIAS, or-- or-- or Joint, or anything else. I remember we were borrowing some money from the JIAS. Do you know what the JIAS is? They used to be on Beverly Street. And I wanted $400, and they said, you have to pay $8 back. And I said, listen, I have only a job which only pays $31.70. I cannot pay you $8 back. And he says, well, if you cannot pay $8, how much can you? I said, $4. He said, then, we give you $250, instead of $400.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not holding it against it. It was-- it was interest-free loan, and they-- you see, now, when a loaf of bread is $2.09, and, at that time, a loaf of bread was $0.17-- $0.17 for a loaf of bread-- I don't blame them. It was a lot of money, $250. And this was given by Jews who had money, and they were handing the whole thing for free. I mean, now, I-- I know that the Jewish Congress is paying people $50,000 or $60,000 from the donations what I'm giving to Israel. I mean, this wasn't like this in those days. People were doing it differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my-- what did I do? I did buy a house, because I knew my father sent out some money in the '30s. And as I told you, we got it back from the bank-- like 2/3 was for me, 1/3 for the men who got it. And altogether, it was about $3,000-some American dollars, which, at that time, was worth 10% more in Canadian. So it means $3,000 American dollars, so 3,300 Canadian dollars.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we paid into a house, and a very, very lousy house. The basement was full of water because there was a creek running underneath it, causing every spring, the creek was re-activated. So we were living there for three and a half years, and then we sold it to somebody else. I wasn't a selfish person. I thought somebody who wants to float in the basement, I don't deny it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you teach your son about your experiences? Did you talk to him about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your experiences in-- in Hungary--

speaker: interviewee
text: To whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: --during-- to your son, your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He knows more or less everything. He is telling stories-- you know, the story-- storytelling in Toronto? No. He's a storyteller, and he is telling my story to the people. Now, he said he's telling-- telling my story much better than I do, because he has the experience how to tell a story. I just tell him the story, but he's telling it to the people. So he's very-- [PAUSES] he's very proud of it, that I went through all these things and he's telling to other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think was the most important thing that helped you survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The most important thing to survive?

speaker: interviewer
text: That helped you to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: During, or after?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Important things to survive? Well, many times, when I'm in trouble, I wish I wouldn't have survived, because once I'm not-- if I wouldn't have survived, I wouldn't have the trouble. But we were quite successful. My wife is a very industrious woman, and we were in business for a long time. And I am not a spender, and neither is she. She's not the kind of a woman who is pressing the husband for more money, and more money, more money. She is satisfied when we have money to-- not only as much money, how much we need for a living, but excess money so in case there is going to be-- there are going to be rainy days. Do you remember I told you about the royal blue-- blue--

speaker: interviewer
text: Turtleneck.

speaker: interviewee
text: --turtleneck sweater? So this is the attitude. Maybe we will never need it, but it's good to have it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's my attitude. I'm not a very-- [PAUSES] not a talented person. Instead of talent, I'm-- I'm-- I'm a saver. So because I know the money, what you can make today, has to be saved, because there may be no opportunity to save the money tomorrow. So there are some people who are very talented. They can earn money, and they are spending money, because they know, I am very talented, so I can spend this money today, because tomorrow I'm going to be able to make more money. On the other hand, I said, I'm not a talented man, so whatever money I have, I have to save for tomorrow. So that's it. Do you have a message that you want to say to your son, or to your grandchildren on tape? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They should have born to a Jewish man, should have looked for-- for-- for-- for a Gentile person, and they would have solved their problem. My-- all my-- my son and two older grandchildren are having a very, very strong Jewish feeling. Now, both of them-- now, the one just came back yesterday from Israel, and the other one was in Israel last spring for about four month. And he is not doing anything but involving himself in Jewish affairs-- the older-- oldest one. So he's really, really a very, very good Jew. He's not a-- he is-- he says he is kosher for about a year. But it's one of those-- I don't believe that you have to be kosher in order to prove that you are Jewish. I think a person who is donating money to Israel, who is working for Jewish causes, they are many times better than Mr. [? Dinitz, ?] who is under arrest in Israel, who was stealing money. So I don't know who is more dedicated, the person who is kosher or who is honest. Now, I have a friend who said-- not a friend, some people we know for the past 25 years. He was a butcher, and he was a very, very religious person, and he was taking the extra something off the meat, which would made it more kosher than the other butchers. This man fixed his scale in such a manner that it was showing two ounces more than what it was. To me, this man, no matter how religious, how kosher he was, he wasn't an honest man. And I don't think, if there is a God, who said, well, OK, just go and [? dive ?] [? in, ?] and then cheat your fellow Jew. I-- he wasn't doing-- that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your belief in God--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --change over--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There is no-- when I was a kid, I was taught religion. And they said, it's God, and went to the synagogue. And I went through all the motions, that there is going to be-- I'm going to be punished. I'm going to be done. I said, what kind of a God is that, who is always punishing people? I don't want to be punished. I mean, if this is the kind of a God we have, he can keep his punishments for himself. So-- but I am a Jew. I don't-- I-- just because I don't believe in God doesn't mean that I don't believe in Israel, I don't believe in the benefit of another Jew. I'm donating money to Israel. I-- I'm not cheating anybody-- not a Jew, nor a Gentile. My idea about being a religious person is a person who is not cheating on his fellow [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] man. Not only fellow Jewish man, his Gentile man, or any kind of a man. I'm paying my taxes to the government. I don't try-- whatever is legal, I'm going to deduct. What it is not legal, I'm going to pay. I just-- only thing is, I don't want trouble. I just want to go to sleep and be able to sleep through the night, instead of being afraid that something is going to happen to me, because people are going to discover this, or that, or everything. By the way, the reason I was volunteering to tell my story is because there is a fellow in England, David Irving, there is a person in Moncton, Ross, there is a Keegstra-- Keegstra name-- a person named Keegstra in Alberta, and there's a Zundel in Toronto, who denying the Holocaust. There was a Holocaust. I was-- I was there, and I've seen it. I experienced it. My parents were killed in the Holocaust. So did all my relatives, and many of our friends, who perished in the Holocaust. I just want this to be noted, that there was a Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wake up. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm all set.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello, my name is Andrew Meles. I'm the son of George and Eleanor Meles. I can remember hearing about the Holocaust ever since-- ever since I was a child, even though I didn't know the specifics of it. And I think that that knowledge of the Holocaust by me has spread on to my children, who, behind me, on my right, are Anthony-- and Anthony just yesterday came back from a three-week visit to Israel, where he's worked with Livnot, and did some volunteer work there. And Anthony is also eager to go back to Israel as soon as possible, possibly for four months next summer to do more volunteer work and more studying. Behind me, directly behind me, next to Anthony, is Joshua Meles, who also spent four months in Israel last summer. And Joshua has also participated in the March of the Living a few years ago. Beside Joshua is Jordan. Jordan is 14, Joshua is 20, and Anthony is-- Joshua's 21-- and Anthony's 20. Jordan has the good fortune of being here this evening and missing his religious school studies, because of the-- because of the taping. I believe that my parents' experiences during the war have influenced us all in some way. But instead of just being negative about it, I think we've been proactive in it. I studied political science in university. I've run for public office twice. And my motivation had to do with this experience that I'd heard from my parents. Perhaps some people might think this is too idealistic, but I ran for public office because I thought I would try to make this a better world. My two older boys are also involved in similar sorts of things. They're both studying political science at university. And Joshua's involved in quite a few political activities, belongs to several political organizations that, I believe, he has the same motivation as I have, not only to make this a better world, and a safe-- but also a safer world for Jews. And Joshua is particularly motivated along Zionist lines, and along areas of Jewish interest. The interesting thing about Jewish interest is that, growing up, I--I was always conscious that I was Jewish, though I believe my family is atheistic. I was never taken to a synagogue by my parents. My parents did not take off high holidays. We ate bread on Passover. We had meals on Yom Kippur. When I had children and joined a Reformed temple, my parents were somewhat surprised that-- that I went to this effort, and to this expense, to do this. Yet, they always wanted me to be Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in some ways, I was-- I am more observant in some small way than my parents. And now, I see yet another step on the part of particularly my two older boys, and Joshua in particular, being even more observant-- considerably more observant than I am. Joshua, just over a year ago, about a year and a half ago, had decided to keep kosher, and is being far more traditional in his-- in his Jewish attitude than I am, and more so than my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now? Well, this is my grandfather at the beginning of this century. It was taken in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Adolph, Adolph Meles. M-E-L-E-S. She's my mother-- [? alias, ?] Berta-- Berta, [? alias, ?] at the age of 19 or 20. And taken in Budapest. This is my father, Emanuel Melesz. And was taken in Budapest at age of, I think, 40, more or less. And this is my certificate-- certificate from Mauthausen when I-- where I have been liberated. I have been liberated on May 8 in 1945. But this one says May 28. That's the date of the certificate. There's my fingerprint and my signature.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll just-- I'll just-- I'll just shoot it [? walled. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my letter-- last letter what I have received from my parents. The top one has been written by my father. The bottom is written by my mother. It has been written on June 25, 1944, before they were taken. They were deported-- deported to Mauthausen. And this is the last letter I have received from my fiance, my present wife, before she was taken to Auschwitz. And the date is June 30, 1944.
